title: Separate dissenting opinion of Carmen Salcedo Beltrán: Decision on the merits: Sindacato Autonomo Comitato Nazionale Pompieri (CO.NA.PO.) v. Italy, Complaints No. 214/2022 and No. 215/2022
text: - 19 - European Committee of Social Rights Comité européen des Droits sociaux DECISION ON THE MERITS Adoption: 10 September 2025 Notification: 15 October 2025 Publication: 16 February 2026 Sindacato Autonomo Comitato Nazionale Pompieri (CO.NA.PO.) v. Italy Complaints No. 214/2022 and No. 215/2022 SEPARATE DISSENTING OPINION OF CARMEN SALCEDO BELTRÁN 1. I voted against the Committee’s majority decision, which found that there had been no violation of Article 1§2 and no violation of Article 12§3 read alone or Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3 of the European Social Charter. 2. The Committee noted from the complaint that the situation of the national firefighters corps was discriminatory. There is no doubt as to the unequal treatment to which they were subjected with regard to remuneration and supplementary allowances. This state of affairs is legally established and was implicitly recognised by the Government when it adopted Law No. 183/2010, which aimed to reform labour market regulations and employment conditions and came into force on 24 November 2010. 3. However, it was not until 10 years later, with the approval of Decree-Law No. 76 of 16 July 2020, that the Government addressed this discriminatory practice. Such a delay cannot be disregarded, particularly as the text did not introduce any transitional measures to remedy the prejudice suffered during that extended period. The Government is the only party responsible for the excessive delays, and it must bear the consequences of any resulting damage. 4. The legal framework discriminated against a specific group. The legislator admitted as much when the reform was adopted in an attempt to resolve this, but ten years passed before the situation was actually rectified. Furthermore, in merely remedying the situation for the future and failing to make retroactive reparations, the legislator took absolutely no account of the harm caused by the delay. 5. The majority of the Committee considered that the delay fell within the Government’s margin of appreciation. At the same time, it found that the absence of retroactive salary equalisation for firefighters who retired before 2020, as well as those still in active service during the 2010-2020 period, did not amount to discrimination within the meaning of the Charter. 6. For my part, I consider and wish to emphasise that this is a violation of the Charter with a long-term effect, and that, consequently, a correction without transitional measures would constitute an ongoing violation of the rights enshrined in the Charter. The Committee should have applied its established case law on jurisdiction ratione temporis (Marangopoulos Foundation for Human Rights v. Greece, Complaint No. 30/2005, decision on admissibility of 10 October 2005, § 15) in line with that of the European Court of Human Rights in Blečić v. Croatia (application no. 59532/00, judgment of 8 March 2006, Grand Chamber, §§63-76) and Šilih v. Slovenia (application no. 71463/01, judgment of 9 April 2009, Grand Chamber, § 146), which were both taken into account in the case of the Centre on Housing Rights and Evictions (COHRE), v. Croatia (Complaint No. 52/2008, decision on the merits of 22 June 2010, §§22-26). When assessing whether there has been a violation, the decisive factor is “the exact time of the alleged interference”, i.e. the effects produced by the facts constituting the violation. In the present case, the discriminatory situation persists and will continue to produce effects in the long term, as the group concerned will endure the effects of the discrimination in their salary and pension calculations for many years. 7. I would also like to express my disagreement with the Committee’s broad interpretation of the margin of appreciation afforded to States in relation to the time limit within which they must remedy a situation of discrimination. Certainly, States should be afforded a margin of appreciation in how they apply rights in certain situations. 8. However, the framework for this margin of appreciation must always be defined in a consistent manner and take due account of the legitimate interests involved (S.H. and Others v. Austria, application no. 57813/00, judgment of 3 November 2011, § 100). I do not concur with the Committee’s view that the State has infinite temporal discretion in addressing discriminatory circumstances. This interpretation may be regarded as akin to validating or even overlooking situations in which States only address the issue years or even decades later, having regard only to the actual moment of compliance and taking no account of the detrimental effects experienced by citizens throughout the duration of the infringement. 9. Compliance with the obligations arising from the Charter, and where relevant, from the Committee’s case law, cannot be left to the States’ discretion or incorporated into their margin of appreciation. The rectification of a violation must necessarily be accompanied by compensation for the harm suffered by those affected by the act in question. Otherwise, as in the present case, discrimination is perpetuated. 10. The aim and purpose of the Charter, being a human rights protection instrument, is to protect rights not merely theoretically, but also in fact (International Commission of Jurists (ICJ) v. Portugal, Complaint No. 1/1998, decision on the merits of 9 September 1999, § 32). In the instant case, the 10-year delay in rectifying the discriminatory practices is both unjustified and especially long. The absence of transitional measures not only exacerbated the damage suffered by the affected group, but also undermined the effectiveness of the guaranteed right. The discrimination has not been rectified; it persists and will continue to have an impact on those affected. 11. For all these reasons, I consider that the Committee should have found a violation of Article 1§2 and Article 12§3 read alone or Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3 of the Charter.
